content:
# Acts Chapter 11 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 238-239 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_011_page_001.jpg, ACT_011_page_002.jpg

---

**1.** And the apostles and the brothers who were throughout Judea heard that also the Gentiles received the word of God.

**2.** And when Peter went up to Jerusalem, those of [the] circumcision were disputing with him,

**3.** saying that, "You went in to men having uncircumcision and ate with them."

**4.** And Peter having begun was explaining to them in order, saying,

**5.** "I was in [the] city of Joppa praying, and I saw in an ecstasy a vision, a certain vessel descending, as a great linen-sheet by four corners being let down out of the heaven, and it came unto me.

**6.** Into which having looked intently, I was considering, and I saw the four-footed [animals] of the earth and the wild-beasts and the creeping-things and the birds of the heaven.

**7.** And I also heard a voice saying to me, 'Having risen, Peter, slaughter and eat.'

**8.** And I said, 'By no means, Lord, because [anything] common or unclean has never entered into my mouth.'

**9.** And a voice answered a second [time] out of the heaven, 'What God has cleansed, you shall not make common.'

**10.** And this happened upon three [times], and all was drawn up again into the heaven.

**11.** And behold, immediately three men stood at the house in which I was, having been sent from Caesarea to me.

**12.** And the Spirit told me to go with them, doubting nothing. And these six brothers also came with me, and we entered into the man's house.

**13.** And he reported to us how he saw the angel standing in his house and saying, 'Send to Joppa and send-for Simon who is called Peter,

**14.** who will speak words to you by which you will be saved, you and all your household.'

**15.** And as I began to speak, the Holy Spirit fell upon them just as also upon us in [the] beginning.

**16.** And I remembered the word of the Lord, how he was saying, 'John indeed baptized [with] water, but you will be baptized in [the] Holy Spirit.'

**17.** If then God gave the equal gift to them as also to us, having believed upon the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I [to be] able to hinder God?"

**18.** And having heard these [things], they were silent and glorified God, saying, "Then also to the Gentiles God has given the repentance unto life."

**19.** Those indeed therefore who were scattered from the tribulation having happened over Stephen passed through as far as Phoenicia and Cyprus and Antioch, speaking the word to no one except only [to] Jews.

**20.** And there were some of them, men of Cyprus and Cyrene, who having come into Antioch were speaking also to the Greeks, proclaiming-the-good-news [of] the Lord Jesus.

**21.** And [the] hand of [the] Lord was with them, and a great number having believed turned to the Lord.

**22.** And the word was heard in the ears of the church being in Jerusalem concerning them, and they sent out Barnabas as far as Antioch.

**23.** Who having arrived and having seen the grace of God, rejoiced, and was exhorting all with purpose of heart to remain in the Lord.

**24.** Because he was a good man and full of [the] Holy Spirit and of faith. And a considerable crowd was added to the Lord.

**25.** And he went out to Tarsus to seek-out Saul,

**26.** and having found [him], he brought [him] to Antioch. And it came-to-pass [that] for a whole year they were gathered together in the church and taught a considerable crowd, and [that] the disciples were called Christians first in Antioch.

**27.** And in these days prophets came down from Jerusalem to Antioch.

**28.** And having stood up, one of them by name Agabus was signifying through the Spirit [that] a great famine was about to be upon the whole inhabited-earth, which happened under Claudius.

**29.** And of the disciples, according as any [of them] was prospering, each of them determined to send [something] for ministry to the brothers dwelling in Judea;

**30.** which also they did, having sent to the elders through [the] hand of Barnabas and Saul.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**Verse 2:** Sinaiticus reads οἱ ἐκ περιτομῆς ("those of the circumcision"), identifying Peter's critics as the circumcision party specifically, consistent with the broader manuscript tradition.

**Verse 3:** Sinaiticus preserves the accusative construction ἄνδρας ἀκροβυστίαν ἔχοντας ("men having uncircumcision"), a characteristically blunt Lukan rendering of the Jewish critique.

**Verse 6:** Sinaiticus includes καὶ τὰ θηρία ("and the wild-beasts") in the list of creatures in the vessel, which is absent from the parallel account in Acts 10:12 in some witnesses. This addition here is distinctive of Peter's retelling.

**Verse 11:** Sinaiticus reads ἐν ᾗ ἤμην ("in which I was") for the house, and specifies τρεῖς ἄνδρες ("three men"), consistent with the narrative of 10:7 (two servants and a soldier).

**Verse 12:** Sinaiticus reads μηδὲν διακρίναντα ("doubting nothing" / "making no distinction"), where the participle is accusative singular, referring to Peter himself. Some manuscripts read the nominative διακρίνοντα.

**Verse 17:** Sinaiticus reads τὴν ἴσην δωρεάν ("the equal gift"), emphasizing the identical nature of the Spirit's bestowal on Gentiles and Jews. The phrase ἐπιστεύσασιν ἐπί ("having believed upon") takes the dative participle.

**Verse 19:** Sinaiticus preserves the connection to the Stephen persecution (Acts 8:1-4) with the phrase τῆς θλίψεως τῆς γενομένης ἐπὶ Στεφάνῳ ("the tribulation having happened over Stephen").

**Verse 20:** Sinaiticus reads Ἑλληνιστάς ("Hellenists" / "Greeks"), a significant textual variant. Some manuscripts read Ἕλληνας ("Greeks" proper, i.e., Gentiles). The distinction matters greatly: Ἑλληνιστάς could mean Greek-speaking Jews, while Ἕλληνας means ethnic Greeks/Gentiles. The context of the narrative (contrasting with preaching "only to Jews" in v.19) strongly favors the sense of Gentile Greeks, and Sinaiticus may preserve an early reading that was later clarified in other witnesses.

**Verse 22:** Sinaiticus reads ἐξαπέστειλαν ("they sent out/forth") with the compound prefix ἐξ-, emphasizing the official commissioning of Barnabas.

**Verse 26:** Sinaiticus attests the famous notice that Χριστιανούς ("Christians") was first used as a name in Antioch. The verbal form χρηματίσαι ("were called" / "were designated") carries an official or public connotation.

**Verse 28:** Sinaiticus reads ἐπὶ Κλαυδίου ("under Claudius"), the historical note identifying the famine with the reign of Emperor Claudius (AD 41-54). Josephus corroborates a severe famine in Judea circa AD 46-47.

**Verse 30:** The chapter closes with the relief mission, linking Barnabas and Saul as the authorized agents delivering aid to the Jerusalem elders (πρεσβυτέρους), the first mention of this office in Acts.

-- EMET (אֱמֶת)
